As you follow the old man to his shack, you notice the smell of salt in the air and hear the gentle lapping of the waves against the dock. The shack is quaint and cozy, filled with the warmth of a crackling fire in the hearth. The old man rummages around for a moment before turning to you with a smile that crinkles the corners of his eyes. "I couldn't just let such potential go to waste," he says, handing you a beautifully crafted fishing rod. It feels sturdy in your hands, the wood polished smooth and the line supple and strong. "Consider this a gift, young one. May it serve you well in your adventures ahead." The old man then gestures towards a small selection of fishing supplies on a nearby table. Among them, you see some colorful lures, a sturdy net, and a box of shiny hooks. Each item looks well-made and ready for use. As you thank the old man for his generosity, you feel a surge of gratitude and excitement at the prospect of exploring the waters around the city with your new gear. TAVERN STORY PART You enter the Izakaya, the tavern in town, and are greeted by the warm glow of lanterns hanging from the ceiling, casting a soft light over the wooden tables and chairs. The smell of hearty food and ale fills the air, mingling with the sound of lively chatter and clinking glasses. The bartender, a jovial man with a welcoming smile, motions for you to take a seat at the bar. As you settle onto a stool, you notice a group of students from the university huddled at a nearby table, their laughter and animated gestures filling the room. The bartender slides a mug of frothy ale towards you, the cool condensation dripping down its sides. You take a sip, the bitter taste tingling on your tongue. The tavern is a hub of activity, with travelers sharing tales of their adventures, locals discussing the latest gossip, and the occasional bard strumming a tune in the corner. You feel a sense of camaraderie in the air, a feeling of belonging in this bustling establishment. As you enjoy your drink and soak in the lively atmosphere, you overhear snippets of conversations about the university and its students with special abilities. Curiosity piques your interest, and you wonder what kind of talents these individuals possess. The Izakaya seems like the perfect place to gather information and maybe even make some new friends before heading to the university. The bartender approaches you, wiping a glass with a cloth, and asks if you're new in town. You nod, and he offers you a plate of steaming food, a gesture of hospitality. As you dig in, you can't help but feel grateful for stumbling upon this welcoming tavern, a place where you can rest, refuel, and maybe even uncover more about the mysterious world you've found yourself in.

Error at (18, 31): Too many arguments for "move_and_slide()" call. Expected at most 0 but received 1.   Ah! That error is because you’re using Godot 4, and in Godot 4, CharacterBody2D changed its movement functions.  In Godot 3, move_and_slide(velocity) was correct. In Godot 4, you don’t pass velocity to move_and_slide anymore — instead, you set velocity on the CharacterBody2D and call move_and_slide() with no arguments.  Here’s the fixed Player.gd for Godot 4:  extends CharacterBody2D  @export var speed = 200  func _physics_process(delta):     var input_vector = Vector2.ZERO      if Input.is_action_pressed("ui_right"):         input_vector.x += 1     if Input.is_action_pressed("ui_left"):         input_vector.x -= 1     if Input.is_action_pressed("ui_down"):         input_vector.y += 1     if Input.is_action_pressed("ui_up"):         input_vector.y -= 1      input_vector = input_vector.normalized() * speed     velocity = input_vector     move_and_slide()  ✅ Key Changes for Godot 4  Use velocity property instead of passing it to move_and_slide().  move_and_slide() is now called with no arguments.  Everything else (input, speed) stays the same.  If you replace your script with this version, that error will disappear, and your player will move properly in Godot 4. Invalid assignment of property or key 'text' with value of type 'String' on a base object of type 'null instance'.  Ah! That error is very common in Godot when your script tries to access a node that doesn’t exist yet or the path is wrong. The message:  Invalid assignment of property or key 'text' with value of type 'String' on a base object of type 'null instance'.   means that message_label is null — the script couldn’t find $Label.  Let’s fix it step by step.  1️⃣ Check Your Scene Hierarchy  For the UI script to work, you need this exact structure:  UI (CanvasLayer)  <-- Script attached here └─ Label          <-- Name must be EXACTLY "Label"   ✅ Common mistakes that break it:  Label is not a child of UI (it’s a sibling or under another node)  Label’s name is not exactly Label (e.g., label or Label2)  The script runs before the node is ready (rare if using _ready() + $Label)  2️⃣ Correct Script for UI  Here’s a safe version that won’t crash even if the node is missing, and will give you a warning instead:  extends CanvasLayer  @onready var message_label = $Label  # make sure Label is a child of UI and named "Label"  func _ready():     if message_label == null:         push_error("Label node not found under UI! Check node hierarchy.")         return      show_message("Welcome to the RPG!")  func show_message(text: String):     if message_label != null:         message_label.text = text   ✅ Check:  UI has Label as a direct child  Label’s name is exactly Label  Run the scene → "Welcome to the RPG!" appears  3️⃣ Debugging Tip  If you still get null instance:  Click on UI → expand children → see if Label is really there  Rename it to exactly Label (capital L)  Save the scene, then run  Once this is fixed, the UI will work without crashing, and we can safely add NPCs, dialogue, and battle nodes next.
